  • The 800G NDR module’s core chip utilizes Broadcom VCSEL lasers and a Broadcom 7nm DSP chip.
  • The VCSEL laser array boasts high-frequency response, enabling stable 8x100G PAM4 signal output, ensuring signal integrity across a total bandwidth of 800Gbps and minimizing latency losses during signal transmission.
  • The 7nm DSP chip adopts an advanced process, which not only improves the PAM4 signal processing efficiency but also significantly reduces power consumption, providing core support for the overall low-power design of the OSFP transceivers.

3. Efficient Package Heat Dissipation Design

  • Dual MPO-12 interfaces: Support 800G → 2×400G splitting, seamlessly compatible with ConnectX-7 adapters and IB NDR systems .
  • The OSFP transceiver utilizes a Finned Top package, shortening the heat transfer path from the chip to the heat sink, ensuring module power consumption ≤14W for long-term stable operation and preventing latency fluctuations caused by high temperatures.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

4. Multiple Dimension Tests to Ensure InfiniBand Network Reliability

  • Maintaining low BER even in extreme scenarios, the pre-FEC BER can reach 1E-8 to 1E-10, and post-FEC transmission is error-free, meeting the stringent “zero packet loss” requirements of AI training and high-frequency trading.
  • Support for LL-FEC (272,257+1) compresses latency to sub-microseconds, breaking the performance bottleneck of RS-FEC and achieving true deterministic latency (<1μs) in large clusters, eliminating network “long-tail latency.”

To dive deeper into how FEC enhances InfiniBand network performance and why LL-FEC is crucial for next-generation AI and HPC clusters, check out our detailed blog: Forward Error Correction (FEC) Enhances InfiniBand Network Performance

Diversified Solution to Unleash the Efficiency of Large Clusters

NADDOD OSFP 800G 2xSR4/SR8 optical modules support flexible link configurations to meet interconnection requirements in various scenarios:

  • 800G Full Bandwidth Solution: A single 800G InfiniBand module directly provides an 800Gbps link, suitable for direct “switch-to-switch” connections in AI 10,000-card clusters, eliminating intermediate transfer links and reducing latency.
  • 2×400G Split Solution: Splits an 800G link into two 400G links using dual MPO-12 interfaces. This solution is suitable for hybrid networking scenarios with a 400G NIC and an 800G switch. This solution helps customers smoothly transition to an 800G architecture while deploying a large number of existing 400G networking devices, protecting existing investments and gradually upgrading port density and bandwidth.

Product Advantages: Supporting Lossless Expansion in Large Clusters

In AI clusters with 10,000 or 100,000 AI cards, the core advantages of the OSFP 800G 2xSR4/SR8 optical transceivers are concentratedly reflected:

  • Doubled bandwidth significantly improves efficiency: The 800Gbps bandwidth per port doubles the 400Gbps bandwidth of existing solutions, accelerating inter-GPU gradient synchronization and inter-node data exchange. In typical AI training tasks, the Wanka cluster reduces single-round iteration time from approximately 2.1 seconds to 1.4 seconds, shortening the overall training cycle by several weeks and accelerating model rollout.
  • Lossless transmission ensures cluster stability: Support for LL-FEC (272,257+1) compresses latency to sub-microseconds, effectively reducing training rollbacks and task failures caused by link interruptions. In large-scale HPC operations, cluster stability is significantly improved and node failure rates are significantly reduced.
  • Topology compatibility and support for large-scale scalability: The 800G optical module is compatible with InfiniBand NDR and the related ecosystem at the protocol level, supporting deployment in multi-level topologies such as DragonFly+ and Clos. Its large port bandwidth and lossless transmission mechanism enable networks to maintain low hop counts and low congestion even when scaling to tens of thousands of nodes, ensuring near-linear performance improvements as cluster scales.

Conclusion

As an optical network solutions provider, NADDOD has solid technical capabilities and practical experience in the planning, construction, and optimization of InfiniBand high-performance networks. We offer comprehensive interconnect solutions, including high-performance InfiniBand switches , HCAs , latest Infiniband XDR transceiver and cables , Infiniband NDR and HDR products , tailored to specific application scenarios. We build high-bandwidth, low-latency, and scalable network infrastructure for data centers, HPC, edge computing, and AI systems, ensuring performance while effectively reducing deployment and maintenance costs. If you want to learn more about InfiniBand 800G optical modules , please visit NADDOD.com or contact our experts directly.

item.title: InfiniBand XDR Networking Product Guide and Optical Connectivity Solutions
item.description:
Over the past two years, the scale of generative AI has grown exponentially, with the number of parameters leaping from hundreds of billions to trillions. This has placed unprecedented demands on computing power, data exchange capabilities, and overall cluster economics. To support such massive training tasks, AI clusters have rapidly expanded in scale, growing from hundreds of GPU nodes to tens of thousands of nodes. However, as computing power increases, issues such as network transmission latency, insufficient bandwidth, deployment and operational costs, and energy consumption pressures are increasingly becoming key bottlenecks constraining cluster efficiency and sustainable development. To address these challenges, NVIDIA Quantum-XDR InfiniBand offers a high-performance, cost-effective high-speed interconnect solution for hyperscale AI clusters, featuring transmission rates of up to 1.6 Tbps, ultra-low latency, and high energy efficiency.

NVIDIA Quantum-X800 XDR InfiniBand Switch

Q3400-RA

The NVIDIA Quantum-X800 Q3400-RA is a 4U rack-mounted InfiniBand switch featuring 200Gb/s SerDes technology per channel, with 144 ports capable of delivering up to 800Gb/s of port bandwidth. It can connect up to 10,368 ConnectX-8 network interface cards (NICs) with minimal latency, meeting the needs of data centers of various scales. Additionally, it provides a high-performance, highly reliable underlying network architecture for training and deploying trillion-parameter-scale generative AI models, accelerating the evolution of intelligent computing centers toward higher computational density and completely resolving bandwidth bottlenecks and latency issues in large-scale AI cluster training.

Q3200-RA

The NVIDIA Quantum-X800 Q3200 is a 2U air-cooled InfiniBand switch that is ideal for small to medium-sized platforms or scenarios requiring integration with existing infrastructure. The system includes two independent switches in a single chassis, each providing 36 800Gb/s ports. The Q3200 fixed-configuration switch is ideal for connecting new compute clusters to previous-generation Quantum and Quantum-2 InfiniBand storage infrastructure, providing users with a flexible and efficient solution during network upgrades and expansions.

NVIDIA InfiniBand HCAs

The NVIDIA ConnectX-8 SuperNIC is optimized to significantly improve the processing capabilities of hyperscale AI computing workloads. The product supports both InfiniBand and Ethernet protocols with transfer rates of up to 800 Gb/s, providing high-speed, efficient network connectivity that significantly improves system performance for AI factories and cloud data centers.

NVIDIA C8180

The NVIDIA C8180 is part of the NVIDIA ConnectX-8 series SuperNIC network cards. It features a single-port OSFP design with an HHHL configuration, operating by default in 800Gb/s XDR IB mode while also supporting 2x400GbE. This NIC supports encryption and secure boot to ensure the security of data transmission and system operation.

NVIDIA C8240

The NVIDIA C8240 is part of the NVIDIA ConnectX-8 series SuperNIC network cards. Featuring an HHHL form factor, it fits into corresponding server slots. Operating by default at 400GbE, it also supports the NDR IB networking protocol, enabling flexible deployment across diverse network environments. Equipped with dual QSFP112 ports, the card delivers efficient and adaptable data transmission.

InfiniBand XDR Optical Connectivity Solutions

InfiniBand XDR networking primarily relies on interconnections between XDR switches, servers (network interface cards), and optical connectivity components to achieve ultra-high bandwidth and microsecond latency. This enables extreme performance for ultra-large-scale AI clusters with tens of thousands of cards, shortening model training cycles and reducing computing costs.

To ensure stable interconnection within cabinets, between cabinets, and across data centers for 10,000-card-scale XDR clusters, DAC, active copper cables, and single-mode optical transceivers + patch cords have become indispensable infrastructure. They not only carry high-speed data streams of 200 Gb/s per channel but also ensure the reliability of the entire AI network through extremely low error rates and low latency designs.

Single-mode Optical Transceivers — OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H

NADDOD OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H is a silicon photonics-based parallel 8-channel optical transceiver compatible with InfiniBand and Ethernet protocols, delivering a total bandwidth of 1.6Tb/s. Featuring a dual-port OSFP packaging design, it achieves a single-port rate of 800Gb/s. The transceiver incorporates two 4-channel MPO-12/APC optical connectors, each capable of 800Gb/s transmission.

This single-mode transceiver is specifically engineered for data center deployments. Utilizing an 8-channel design with 200G-PAM4 modulation technology, it achieves a maximum transmission distance of 500 meters over 8 single-mode fibers.

Applications

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H primarily enables interconnections between Quantum-X800 XDR switches and between Quantum-X800 XDR switches and ConnectX-8 OSFP network cards.

(1) Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — Quantum-X800 XDR Switch

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H dual-port transceiver enables interconnection of Q3400-RA/Q3200-RA switches equipped with OSFP ports at a 1.6T (2×800G) rate via two direct-attach single-mode MPO-12/APC fiber patch cables ( S2MPOA12FB ), supporting a maximum transmission distance of 500 meters.

(2) Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — 2 x ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H dual-port transceiver connects two OSFP-800G-DR4H deployed on the OSFP ports of ConnectX-8 network cards via two direct-attach single-mode MPO-12/APC fiber patch cables (S2MPOA12FB), enabling interconnectivity from 1.6T to 2x800G speeds.

(3) Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — 4 x ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H dual-port transceiver can connect four OSFP-800G-DR4H optical transceivers used on the ConnectX-8 network card side via two 1:2 breakout single-mode MPO-12/APC fiber patch cables ( S2MPOA2x4F ), enabling interconnection from 1.6T to 4x400G speeds. (The OSFP-800G-DR4H can be throttled down to 400G by enabling only two ports.)

Single-mode Optical Transceivers — OSFP-1.6T-2xFR4H

NADDOD OSFP-1.6T-2xFR4H is a 1.6T dual-port OSFP optical transceiver supporting 2×800Gb/s rates with an 8-channel multiplexed design. The transceiver features two dual-fiber LC duplex connectors, each carrying 4x 200G-PAM4 signals. This long-reach 8-channel (2×FR4) transceiver employs 200G-PAM4 optoelectronic modulation technology based on CWDM4 serial multiplexing with a 1310nm wavelength grid. It is specifically designed for next-generation 1.6Tbps data center communication applications and InfiniBand protocol scenarios.

Applications

The OSFP-1.6T-2xFR4H primarily enables interconnectivity between Quantum-X800 XDR switches, supporting transmission distances up to 2km.

Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — Quantum-X800 XDR Switch

The OSFP-1.6T-2xFR4H dual-port transceiver enables interconnection of Q3400-RA/Q3200-RA switches equipped with OSFP ports at a 1.6T (2×800G) rate via two direct-attach single-mode LC duplex fiber patch cables ( S2LUD ), supporting a maximum transmission distance of 2km.

Single-mode Optical Transceivers — OSFP-800G-DR4H

NADDOD OSFP-800G-DR4H is an 800Gb/s single-mode optical transceiver supporting the XDR 800Gb/s InfiniBand protocol. It employs 4-channel PMA4 signal modulation with a per-channel rate of 200Gb/s. This transceiver enables interconnects between Quantum-X800 QM3x00 switches and ConnectX-8 SuperNICs, supporting two data rates: 800Gb/s and 400Gb/s, depending on the fiber patch cable type used. When deployed over single-mode fiber MTP/MPO-12 APC cables, the OSFP DR4 achieves a maximum distance of 500 meters.

Applications

(1) Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — 2 x ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H dual-port transceiver, when used with the Q3400-RA switch and paired with two direct-attach single-mode MPO-12/APC fiber patch cables (S2MPOA12FB), supports two ConnectX-8 OSFP SuperNICs. each equipped with an OSFP-800G-DR4H transceiver, achieving a maximum transmission distance of 500 meters.

(2)Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — 4 x ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs

The OSFP-1.6T-2xDR4H dual-port transceiver is deployed on the QuantumX-800 QM3200 switch. Using two 1:2 breakout MPO fiber patch cables (S2MPOA2x4F), it can support up to four 400G ConnectX-8 SuperNICs. The OSFP ports on the ConnectX-8 NIC side utilize OSFP-800G-DR4H optical transceivers.

DAC Direct-Connect Passive Copper Cable — OSFP-1.6T-CU0–9H

NADDOD OSFP-1.6T-CU0–9H is a 1,600 Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/finned top) to 1,600 Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/finned top) passive direct attach copper (DAC) cable. Due to its simple design and minimal components, this DAC cable offers low cost, ultra-low latency, and near-zero power consumption for high-speed interconnects. It uses the OSFP form factor and contains eight high-speed electrical copper pairs, each supporting data rates of up to 200 Gb/s, enabling short-distance transmission of up to 0.9 m.

Applications

The OSFP-1.6T-CU0–9H is primarily used to enable short-distance interconnections between QuantumX-800 Q3400-RA/Q3200-RA switches, providing a 1.6T interconnect speed.

ACC Direct-Attach Active Copper Cable — OSFP-1.6T-AC11FH

NADDOD OSFP-1.6T-AC11FH is a 1600Gb/s dual-port OSFP (RHS/flat top) to 1600Gb/s dual-port OSFP (RHS/flat top) active cable (ACC). This active copper cable employs 8-channel 200G-PAM4 modulation technology, supporting transmission distances up to 1.1 meters. With its simplified design and minimal component count, the Active Copper Cable (ACC) represents a low-cost, low-latency, low-power, high-speed link solution, second only to passive DAC.

Applications

The OSFP-1.6T-AC11FH ACC cable lacks heat sinks and is therefore incompatible with the OSFP ports on QuantumX-800 series switches (which require heat-sink-equipped cables). Consequently, it is primarily used for short-distance interconnects between ConnectX-8 OSFP SuperNICs.

ACC Direct-Attach Active Copper Cable — OSFP-1.6T-AC11H

NADDOD OSFP-1.6T-AC11H is a 1,600 Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/finned top) to 1,600 Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/finned top) active copper cable (ACC). This active copper cable employs 8-channel 200G-PAM4 modulation technology. Due to its simple design and minimal components, the ACC represents a low-cost, low-latency, and low-power high-speed link solution, second only to passive DAC.

Applications

The OSFP-1.6T-AC11H is primarily used to interconnect QuantumX-800 Q3400-RA/Q3200-RA switches, providing low-cost, low-power, and low-latency interconnection.

1:2 ACC Active Copper Splitter Cable — O2O224–1.6T-AC1H

NADDOD O2O224–1.6T-AC1H is a 1600Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/fin-top) to 2x800Gb/s OSFP (RHS/flat-top) active copper splitter cable (ACC). This active copper cable employs 8-channel 200G-PAM4 modulation technology with a length range of 1 to 2.5 meters. Featuring a simplified design with minimal components, the Active Copper Cable (ACC) represents a low-cost, low-latency, low-power high-speed link solution, second only to passive DAC.

Applications

Quantum-X800 XDR Switch — — 2 x ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs

O2O224–1.6T-AC1H ACC cable with dual ports for 2x800G connections to the QuantumX-800 switch, with two breakout ports for 800G connections to ConnectX-8 SuperNICs, enabling connectivity between 1.6T and 2x800G speeds.

1:4 ACC Active Copper Splitter Cable — O4O224–1.6T-AC1H

NADDOD O4O224–1.6T-AC1H is a 1600Gb/s dual-port OSFP (IHS/fin-top) to 4×400Gb/s OSFP (RHS/flat-top) active splitter cable (ACC) featuring 8-channel 200G-PAM4 modulation, with a length range of 1 to 2.5 meters. With its streamlined design and minimal components, the ACC cable delivers low-cost, low-latency, and low-power advantages comparable to passive DAC while maintaining high-speed interconnect performance. It is ideally suited for high-bandwidth, low-power interconnect requirements in data centers.

Applications

O4O224–1.6T-AC1H ACC cable with dual ports for 2x800G connections to QuantumX-800 switches, featuring four branch ports for 400G connections to ConnectX-8 XDR SuperNICs, enabling connectivity between 1.6T and 2x400G speeds.

NADDOD offers a comprehensive range of optical and electrical connectivity solutions for Infiniband XDR clusters. These solutions provide ultra-high speeds, low error rates, and excellent thermal performance for AI networks, featuring superior signal integrity and reliability, and are fully compatible with NVIDIA devices to ensure the long-term efficient and stable operation of large-scale AI clusters. To guarantee the reliability of the device, we carried out interoperability tests with NVIDIA MMS4A00 and compatibility tests with Quantum-X800 switches , confirming seamless integration across the ecosystem.

XDR Networking vs NDR Networking: Hardware, Cost, and Performance

In AI large-scale model training and inference, network connectivity directly impacts cluster scalability, performance, and cost. NVIDIA XDR switches offer significant advantages over NDR switches in terms of hardware quantity, cost control, and network performance.

1.Significant Reduction in Networking Hardware

In AI cluster large-scale model training/inference, the more ports a device has, the stronger its networking capability, enabling support for larger-scale cluster expansion. XDR switches feature 144 OSFP 800G ports, while NDR switches have 64 OSFP 400G ports. Under the same cluster scale, XDR switches can significantly reduce the number of hardware devices required.

As shown in the figure below, under the same cluster networking scale, the number of switches, optical transceivers, and patch cables in an XDR network is significantly reduced. At 512 nodes, using an XDR network reduces the number of switches by 86%, optical transceivers by 50%, and patch cables by 66.7%. Reducing the number of devices can significantly lower hardware costs, save power consumption, reduce cabling costs, and decrease operational workload, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the data center.

2.Cost Savings at Scale

In terms of network scalability, XDR Layer 2 networking can scale up to 2,592 nodes/20,000+ GPUs (400G CX7), which is an order of magnitude higher than the NDR solution’s 256 nodes/2,048 GPUs (400G CX7), providing more powerful scalability and a 912.5% improvement in networking capabilities.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

As shown in the figure below, in a large-scale deployment of 512 nodes, XDR significantly reduces total costs (reducing costs by 40.52% compared to NDR), making it suitable for ultra-large-scale AI training/inference clusters with 10,000+ nodes. Combined with XDR’s inherent greater scalability, as the number of nodes increases, XDR’s cost advantages become more pronounced in larger-scale networks.

3.Superior Performance

The 144 800G OSFP ports on XDR switches can support large-scale GPU models and are future-proof, offering a high return on investment. In terms of bandwidth capacity and SHARP technology acceleration, XDR switches far exceed NDR switches, providing AI clusters with a network environment that offers lower latency and higher throughput.

Currently, it is primarily suited for training trillion-parameter large models. In these scenarios demanding extreme performance, it delivers faster convergence, higher efficiency, and stronger scalability for customers.

1.6T XDR Frequently Asked Questions

1.What are the advantages of using 1.6T optical transceiversin data centers?

In data centers, the 1.6T XDR single-mode optical module connects devices via two single-mode patch cables, achieving 2×800G double bandwidth. This not only significantly improves network throughput and GPU cluster interconnect efficiency but also reduces the number of ports and cabling, lowering overall power consumption and operational costs. Additionally, it supports backward compatibility and future scalability, providing large-scale AI/HPC clusters with higher performance, greater energy efficiency, and a more forward-looking network foundation.

2.What are the advantages of the 1.6T optical transceiverscompared to 400G/800G optical transceivers?

The advantages of the 1.6T optical module over 400G/800G optical transceivers include: higher bandwidth density, lower power consumption, fewer cables and port requirements, while maintaining backward compatibility and future scalability, significantly reducing the construction and operational costs of large-scale AI/HPC data centers.

3.Which fibers can be used with 1.6T optical transceivers?

Currently, 1.6T single-mode optical transceivers are primarily used with OS2 single-mode patch cords, with a maximum transmission distance of 2km. They are compatible with the cabling solutions of the previous-generation 400G/800G single-mode optical transceivers, allowing for seamless upgrades without changing the fiber patch cords.

4.What is the typical coverage range of 1.6T optical-electrical interconnect products?

The coverage range of 1.6T optical-electrical interconnect products primarily depends on the customer’s application scenario. In intra-cabinet or inter-cabinet interconnections, DAC/ACC is commonly used, making it ideal for back-to-back or intra-cabinet direct connections. For inter-floor or inter-data center interconnections, single-mode transceivers can be used, with options for 500m or 2km based on distance requirements.

By combining copper cables with optical transceivers, the 1.6T interconnection solution can meet different distance requirements, ensuring that AI and high-performance computing clusters deliver excellent bandwidth, latency, and reliability performance across various scenarios.

5.What are the primary application scenarios for 1.6T optical transceivers?

They are used in AI and machine learning, cloud services, and large-scale data centers. NADDOD 1.6T optical transceivers feature ultra-high data rates, ultra-low error rates, and excellent thermal performance, and are fully compatible with NVIDIA devices.

6.What are the common packaging forms for 1.6T optical transceivers?

1.6T optical transceivers primarily use the OSFP packaging form . OSFP is a widely adopted form factor in high-speed interconnections within data centers, offering ample thermal dissipation space and higher power handling capacity, making it suitable for deployment in high-density AI/HPC data centers. The module is typically designed with a dual-port structure, with each port supporting 800G data rates, logically equivalent to integrating two 800G optical transceivers, thereby achieving a single-module 1.6T transmission capacity.

7.What modulation schemes are used in the 1.6T optical module?

The 1.6T optical module uses 200G-PAM4 modulation, with each 800G port composed of 4×200G PAM4 SerDes.

8.What are the mainstream technological approaches for optical components in 1.6T transceivers?

  • Silicon photonics: Suitable for large-scale integration, cost-effective, but limited by laser efficiency.
  • EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser): Mature and reliable, suitable for medium to short distances.
  • Thin-Film Lithium Niobate (TFLN): High-speed, low-loss, gradually entering commercial use.

9.What are the advantages of 1.6T optical transceiverscompared to copper cables (DAC/ACC)?

Optical fibers have low loss and can support high-speed transmission over distances exceeding 500 meters or even further. In contrast, as data rates increase, copper cables experience greater loss during transmission, limiting transmission distances. Additionally, their thicker cable diameter not only occupies cabinet and duct space but also increases the complexity of cabling in large-scale deployments.

item.title: LLM Inference Optimization: NADDOD Joins NVIDIA and Industry Leaders to Validate Prefill-Decode Disaggregation & KV Cache Offload
item.description:
With the rapid implementation of large-scale models in various application scenarios, inference demand continues to grow rapidly. The number of model parameters and concurrent user requests continues to rise, making improving inference performance a core issue of concern in the industry. Among various optimization areas, prefill decode disaggregation improves resource utilization by decoupling compute and communication, while KV cache offload effectively alleviates GPU memory pressure and significantly improves the efficiency of long-text inference optimization through cache offload. To validate the application value of these two key technologies in real-world inference scenarios, NADDOD conducted cross-vendor testing in collaboration with industry leaders such as NVIDIA and Lenovo. During this process, NADDOD provided high-performance network solutions to ensure efficient collaboration between different compute and storage units. This enabled the testing to more efficiently and accurately reflect the performance advantages of prefill decode disaggregation and KV cache offload, providing valuable reference for future large-scale deployments.

Why Conduct This Test?

As large language models (LLMs) continue to grow in size and complexity, the inference side faces two core challenges:

  • Computational efficiency bottleneck: In traditional all-in-one deployment models, executing prefill and decode on the same device leads to severe resource contention. The resource requirements of the two stages cannot be optimized specifically, resulting in low computing resource utilization.
  • Memory bandwidth pressure: KV cache memory overhead scales linearly with context length, making memory bandwidth a performance bottleneck.

Prefill decode disaggregation and KV cache offload are core optimization technologies in current large-model inference, which can significantly improve inference throughput and concurrency. Therefore, cross-vendor joint verification is required to evaluate their actual effects.

Introduction to Prefill Decode Disaggregation and KV Cache Offload Technology

Before delving into the test content, we need to first understand the basic principles of these two technologies and further explore why they are so valuable in LLM inference scenarios.

What is Prefill Decode Disaggregation ?

In the reasoning process of LLM, there are usually two stages:

  • Prefill phase (input processing): The model reads and processes all input tokens in parallel to establish contextual understanding. This phase uses a highly parallel matrix multiplication model, which is computationally intensive but relatively low in memory usage. It is well-suited for high-throughput GPUs and multi-GPU parallel execution.
  • Decode phase (output generation): The model generates tokens one by one based on the context (and the KV cache). This phase uses a sequential generation model, token by token, which is latency-sensitive and rapidly increases in memory usage as the context length increases (due to the need to store the KV cache).

In traditional deployments, these two stages typically run on the same GPUs. However, prefill and decode have completely different resource requirements — one prioritizes high parallel computing power, the other low latency and large video memory. Running them on the same GPUs often compromises performance on both sides.

Prefill decode disaggregation, as the name suggests, deploys prefill and decode separately in different hardware resource pools. The prefill stage uses high-performance, high-throughput GPUs, while the decode stage uses GPUs with larger video memory and lower latency. This approach offers the following benefits:

  • Targeted optimization: Each stage utilizes the most appropriate hardware for optimal performance.
  • Independent resource scalability : Prefill and Decode can independently add or remove GPUs based on load.
  • Improved overall throughput : Avoid one task slowing down another.

Prefill decode disaggregation is a division of labor, allowing prefill and decode to each use the most optimal GPU, thereby improving efficiency and throughput. However, this also requires addressing challenges in communication, scheduling, and memory.

  • Additional communication overhead : If prefill and decode are placed on different GPUs, KV cache must be transferred, and network latency and bandwidth will become bottlenecks.
  • Increased scheduling complexity : The system must coordinate the allocation of the two task columns in real time. Otherwise, the decode stage may wait in vain for prefill results, or prefill resources may be blocked by decode.
  • Implementation complexity : The framework and task scheduler need to support pipelining, kc cache output, and asynchronous execution, making it more difficult to maintain than single-machine, single-GPU inference.
  • High memory usage : The prefill stage may process multiple requests simultaneously, and the decode stage needs to retain the entire KV cache, which will put significant memory pressure.

What is KV Cache Offload ?

If LLM is likened to a novelist, then the Transformer is his or her “thinking method.” When a writer writes a story, they consider their previous writing while deciding what to write next. Similarly, when generating text, the Transformer architecture references previously generated tokens while predicting the next one.

Let’s start with the Transformer. In its self-attention mechanism, each token (which can be thought of as the encoding of a word or character) generates three vectors:

  • Q (Query): What information am I looking for?
  • K (Key): Who am I? (Feature Coordinates)
  • V (Value): What do I bring to the table? (Feature Content)

When Transformer generates text, it only generates one new token at a time. The model uses the Q of this token to match the K of all previous tokens to find the correlation, and then uses these related V to calculate the output. However, the standard Transformer generates tokens one by one during inference, for example:

  • Input: “I want to read a”
  • Generate: “I want to read a book”
  • Continue generating: “I want to read a book”

However, when generating the third token, it recalculates the K and V for all previous tokens, even if this data has already been calculated. This repeated calculation causes the inference computational overhead to grow quadratically with the number of tokens, slowing down the process.

The core principle of KV cache is “don’t reinvent the wheel.” The core idea behind KV cache is to store the previously calculated K and V, eliminating the need for recalculation each time. This way, each time a new token is generated, only the K and V for the new token need to be calculated, added to the previously cached results, and the new token can be generated. This reduces the inference computational overhead from quadratic to linear, significantly accelerating the process.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

While KV cache can accelerate processing, it has the side effect of being very memory-intensive. For example, if a model context length is 80,000 tokens, and the K and V for each token must be stored, the KV cache can occupy tens of GB of GPU memory. However, GPU memory is a valuable resource on GPUs. Besides the KV cache, it also needs to store model weights and intermediate computation results. If GPU memory is full, inference will crash or even fail to run. Offload is to move some data from GPU memory to other storage media, such as:

  • CPU memory (RAM): Large capacity, but slower bandwidth than GPUs
  • SSD: Larger capacity, but slower speeds
  • High-bandwidth memory (HBM) : Fast, but expensive and limited in capacity

In the KV cache scenario, the benefit of offload is that it can move historical KV data that is not currently needed to the CPU/SSD, freeing up GPU memory to ensure that current inference is not stuck. The data can then be moved back when needed (of course, this comes at a cost, so a balance must be struck between speed and memory usage).

Test Overview

1. Test Objectives

Verify the performance optimization effects of prefill decode disaggregation and KV cache offload in LLM, specifically including:

  • Reduce end-to-end latency: Decouple the prefill and decode phases to reduce resource contention.
  • Extend context length: Utilize tiered KV cache storage (GPU memory → host memory/NVMe) to overcome GPU memory capacity limitations.
  • Improve hardware utilization: Optimize the collaboration between compute units and storage media.

2. Test Topology and Environment

See the figure below for the test topology.

This test established a comprehensive environment consisting of a compute cluster, storage nodes, and a high-speed network:

  • Compute Cluster: Multiple high-performance AI servers were deployed, splitting computing power between the prefill and decode phases to compare inference performance across different architectures.
  • Storage System: High-throughput storage nodes were configured to support KV cache offload’s tiered caching and data read/write capabilities.
  • Network Architecture: Both the compute and storage networks are built on a high-bandwidth, low-latency Ethernet solution, ensuring fast data transfer between different units.
  • Software Platform: A mainstream AI operation and scheduling platform was used to manage and monitor the inference process.
  • Optical Interconnect Solution: In this test, NADDOD optical interconnect solution supported high-speed connectivity between the compute and storage networks. Leveraging high-performance, low BER optical transceivers , this solution ensured high-bandwidth transmission and stable interoperability between servers and switches, and switches between switches, ensuring efficient and smooth progress throughout the test.

In the test environment, the overall architecture covers the entire chain of computing, storage, and networking: on the computing side, high-performance GPUs are responsible for prefilling and decoding, and the storage side uses a tiered storage mechanism to efficiently manage KV cache data of different hot and cold levels. On the network side, high-speed interconnection is relied upon to achieve smooth data flow between computing and storage.

3.Test Design

3.1 Prefill Decode Disaggregation Test

This test compares the performance differences between a traditional coupled architecture and a prefill decode disaggregation architecture that decouples compute and communication in LLM inference scenarios.

Key metrics: Prefill latency, decode throughput, and GPU utilization.

3.2 KV Cache Offload Test

This test compares the performance of a full cache-resident GPU and a tiered offload strategy to verify their optimization effectiveness in long-text inference scenarios.

Key metrics: Peak GPU memory usage, offload latency, and maximum supported context length.

3.3 Joint Test

This test verifies the overall effectiveness of the synergistic optimization of prefill decode separation and KV cache offload in real-world conversation scenarios.

Key metrics: End-to-end latency and system energy efficiency.

4. Test Methods and Expected Results Evaluation

In the test environment, the overall architecture, through the division of labor and collaboration between compute and storage units, coupled with tiered storage and high-speed interconnects, fully simulated the load and data flow of real-world large-scale model inference scenarios. Based on this, the effectiveness of prefill decode disaggregation and KV cache offload was verified, and the performance improvement brought by their combined optimization was further evaluated.

  • Validating the effectiveness of prefill decode disaggregation: Using Nsight Systems and perf to analyze latency distribution, comparing the control (coupled architecture) with the experimental (separated architecture) group, we expect a 30%–50% reduction in decode latency and a significant increase in prefill unit utilization.
  • Validating the effectiveness of KV cache offload: Using Prometheus to monitor video memory usage, we expect a reduction of approximately 60% in video memory usage, with context support expanded to 16KB.
  • Validating the effectiveness of the combined optimization: Using the aforementioned tools to measure end-to-end latency and total system power consumption, we expect a 2x increase in total system throughput (QPS) and a 20% reduction in power consumption. The core method is to quantify the actual benefits brought by technology optimization by comparing the key indicators of the experimental group and the control group.

Conclusion

Currently, this cross-manufacturer joint experiment is proceeding in an orderly manner. NADDOD as a participant in this joint test, provided key high-performance network products and solutions, effectively ensuring the high-speed data transmission required for the test and the stable operation of the overall test environment. NADDOD and leading AI industry vendors will, through a rigorous verification process, demonstrate the real-world performance and practical value of combining high-performance networking with prefill decode separation and KV cache offload technologies. This will help optimize large-model inference and achieve higher efficiency in large-scale deployments. Follow NADDOD for ongoing updates on our experiments and technical insights or contact us directly .

item.title: Introduction to RoCE v2 Network. Background
item.description:
Background

NADDOD has a professional technical team and extensive experience in implementing and servicing various application scenarios. The NADDOD technical team believes that RoCE, which enables RDMA functionality over Ethernet, can bypass TCP/IP and utilize hardware offloading to reduce CPU utilization.

There are two main versions of RoCE: RoCEv1 and RoCEv2. RoCEv1 is an RDMA protocol implemented at the Ethernet link layer. Switches need to support flow control technologies like PFC to ensure reliable transmission at the physical layer. RoCEv2 is implemented at the UDP layer of the Ethernet TCP/IP protocol and introduces the IP protocol to address scalability issues.

RoCEv2 supports RDMA routing over Layer 3 Ethernet networks. RoCEv2 replaces the InfiniBand network layer with IP and UDP headers at the Ethernet link layer, enabling routing of RoCE between IP-based traditional routers.

InfiniBand networks are to some extent centrally managed networks with a subnet manager (SM), while RoCEv2 networks are purely distributed networks composed of RoCEv1-supporting NICs and switches, typically adopting a two-tier architecture.

The main suppliers of RoCE network cards are NVIDIA, Intel, and others. PCIe cards are the primary form of network cards for data center servers. The port PHY speed of RDMA cards typically starts from 50Gbps, and currently available commercial network cards can achieve single-port speeds of up to 400Gbps.

Currently, most data center switches support RDMA traffic control technology, which, when combined with RoCE-supporting network cards, enables end-to-end RDMA communication. The core of high-performance switches lies in the forwarding chips they employ. Through in-depth research conducted by the NADDOD professional team, the Tomahawk series chips are widely used in commercial forwarding chips in the current market. Among them, the Tomahawk3 series chips are more commonly used in switches, and the market is gradually seeing an increase in switches that support the Tomahawk4 series chips.

RoCE vs. InfiniBand

Compared to InfiniBand, RoCE offers greater versatility and relatively lower cost. It can be used not only to build high-performance RDMA networks but also in traditional Ethernet environments. However, configuring parameters such as Headroom, PFC (Priority-based Flow Control), and ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) on switches can be complex. In large-scale deployments, the overall throughput performance of RoCE networks may be slightly lower than that of InfiniBand networks.

From a technical perspective, InfiniBand employs various technologies to enhance network forwarding performance, reduce fault recovery time, improve scalability, and lower operational complexity.

In terms of application performance, InfiniBand has lower end-to-end latency compared to RoCEv2, giving InfiniBand-based networks an advantage in application-level performance.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Bandwidth and latency are influenced by factors such as congestion and routing in high-performance network interconnects.

Congestion

InfiniBand utilizes two different frame relay messages to control congestion: Forward Explicit Congestion Notification (FECN) and Backward Explicit Congestion Notification (BECN). When congestion occurs in the network, FECN notifies the receiving device, while BECN notifies the sending device. InfiniBand combines FECN and BECN with adaptive marking rates to reduce congestion. It provides coarse-grained congestion control.

Congestion control on RoCE uses Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN), which is an extension of IP and TCP. It enables endpoint network congestion notification without dropping packets. ECN places a mark on the IP header, indicating the presence of congestion to the sender. For non-ECN congestion communication, the lost packets need to be retransmitted. ECN reduces packet loss caused by TCP congestion, avoiding retransmissions. Fewer retransmissions can reduce latency and jitter, providing better transaction and throughput performance. ECN also provides coarse-grained congestion control but does not have a significant advantage over InfiniBand.

Routing

When congestion occurs in the network, adaptive routing sends devices through alternate routes to alleviate congestion and speed up transmission. RoCE v2 operates on top of IP. Over the years, IP has achieved routability through advanced routing algorithms, and now, with AI machine learning, congestion-aware routing can be predicted, and packets can be automatically sent through faster routes. In terms of routing, Ethernet and RoCE v2 have significant advantages.

However, InfiniBand and RoCE haven’t addressed tail latency much. Tail latency is crucial for synchronous HPC message applications.

UEC Prepares to Define New Transport Protocol

In addition to InfiniBand and RoCE, there have been other protocols proposed in the industry.

On July 19th, the Ultra Ethernet Consortium (UEC) was officially established. UEC aims to go beyond the existing Ethernet capabilities and provide a high-performance, distributed, and lossless transport layer optimized for high-performance computing and artificial intelligence. Founding members of UEC include AMD, Arista, Broadcom, Cisco, Eviden, HPE, Intel, Meta, and Microsoft, all of whom have decades of experience in network, AI, cloud, and high-performance computing deployments.

UEC believes that RDMA, defined decades ago, has become outdated for demanding AI/ML network traffic. RDMA transfers data in large traffic blocks, which can lead to link imbalances and excessive burdens. It is now time to start building a modern transport protocol that supports RDMA for emerging applications.

According to NADDOD, the UEC transport protocol is currently under development with the goal of providing better Ethernet transmission (still supporting RDMA) than the current RDMA. It aims to retain the advantages of Ethernet/IP while delivering the performance required for AI and HPC applications.

UEC transport is a new form close to the transport layer, with some semantic adjustments, congestion notification protocols, and enhanced security features. UEC will provide more flexible transport, eliminating the need for lossless networks, allowing for features such as multi-path and out-of-order packet transmission required for many-to-many AI workloads.

Conclusion

When comparing InfiniBand and RoCE, we can see that both have their own advantages and suitable use cases. IB excels in high-performance computing, offering excellent performance, low latency, and scalability. RoCE, on the other hand, is easier to integrate into existing Ethernet infrastructure and has lower costs. The emerging transport protocols represented by UEC also represent the continuous development and innovation in technology.

Only by adapting to evolving demands can one maintain a competitive edge. As a leading provider of comprehensive optical networking solutions, NADDOD continues to offer innovative, efficient, and reliable products, solutions, and services, providing optimal end-to-end solutions for data centers, high-performance computing, edge computing, artificial intelligence, and other application scenarios. We also continue to learn and research in the fields of InfiniBand, RoCE, and emerging UEC transport protocols, looking forward to breakthroughs in future solutions!

item.title: Understanding the Prefill-decode Disaggregation in LLM Inference Optimization
item.description:
Large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly complex, demanding more efficient inference strategies. One important ai optimization is the prefill decode disaggregation, which decouples the two key stages of inference. Prefill handles the bulk processing of input context, while LLM decode focuses on generating tokens one by one. By separating these stages, we can better utilize hardware resources, reduce latency, and support longer contexts — making large-scale inference optimization more practical and scalable.

1. What is the Prefill and Decode?

In the reasoning process of the Large Language Model (LLM), there are usually two stages:

Prefill Phase (Input Processing)

During this phase, the model reads and processes the entire input text in parallel, breaking it down into tokens and building a holistic understanding of the context. It also generates a key-value (KV) cache to support subsequent generation.

This process is centered around highly parallelized matrix operations, which are computationally intensive but relatively low in memory usage. It is well-suited for running on high-throughput GPUs or multi-GPU systems, efficiently processing input data.

Decode Phase (Output Generation)

During the LLM decode phase, the model enters an autoregressive generation mode: it predicts and outputs new tokens one by one based on the KV cache stored in the LLM prefill phase and the previously generated content. Each new token generated depends on the previous result, making the entire process strictly sequential.

The computational model in this phase is completely different from that of the prefill phase. Compared to the highly parallel large-matrix computations of prefill, decode is more like “word-by-word writing,” generating tokens one by one, making it highly sensitive to latency. As the context expands, the KV cache continues to grow, causing GPU memory usage to rapidly increase.

For this reason, the LLM decode phase is typically much slower than prefill and often underutilizes the GPU. In traditional deployments, the prefill and decode phases often share the same GPUs. However, since the LLM prefill phase prioritizes high throughput computing while the decode phase emphasizes low latency and large GPU memory, mixing them together often limits the performance of both.

2. What is the Prefill–Decode Disaggregation?

As the name suggests, prefill decode disaggregation deploys the two stages of the model in different hardware resource pools to better match their respective computing characteristics.

The prefill stage focuses on high-throughput computing and therefore uses GPUs with high computing power and suitable for large-scale parallel computing. The decode stage, on the other hand, is latency-sensitive and requires larger video memory to store the growing amount of context information. Therefore, GPUs with larger video memory and faster response times are used, thereby improving overall LLM inference optimization efficiency.

(Basic framework of Prefill-Decode separation)

3. The advantages of the Prefill-Decode Disaggregation

In traditional architectures, the LLM prefill and LLM decode stages share the same device, making them prone to interference. Prefill requests can occupy decode resources, leading to increased latency and output lag. Furthermore, the two stages are suited to different parallelization strategies: prefill is suited to tensor parallelism to reduce latency, while decode is suited to pipeline parallelism to increase throughput. This mixed operation is inefficient.

This interference significantly increases the proportion of high-latency decode requests and forces increased hardware investment. Prefill decode separation, by physically separating the two stages, enables more efficient resource utilization and smoother responses.

  • Dedicated Resource Allocation: Prefill and decode can be independently scheduled and scaled on different hardware. For workloads with high overlap (such as multi-turn conversations or proxy processes), large portions of the KV cache can be reused, reducing prefill computational requirements and freeing up resources for the decode phase.
  • Parallel Execution: Prefill and decode no longer block each other and can run simultaneously, achieving higher concurrency and overall throughput.
  • Independent Optimization: Different LLM optimization strategies (such as tensor parallelism or pipeline parallelism) can be applied to prefill and decode, respectively, to better meet the goals of reducing time to first token (TTFT) and LLM inference latency (ITL).

4. The Challenges of Prefill-Decode Separation

Additional Communication Overhead

If LLM prefill and LLM decode are performed on different GPUs, the KV cache must be transferred, and network latency and bandwidth will become bottlenecks.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Solution:

  • High-performance network: Use ultra-low latency, high-bandwidth networks to reduce KV Cache transmission latency between prefill and decode nodes.
  • KV Cache partitioning: Store data in token-based shards, maximizing data proximity to decode nodes to reduce cross-node transmission.

Increased Scheduling Complexity

The system must coordinate the allocation of tasks between the two columns in real time. Otherwise, the LLM decode phase might wait in vain for the prefill results, or the prefill resources might be blocked by the decoding phase.

Solution:

  • Priority Scheduling: LLM Prefill tasks are prioritized to ensure low latency to first token (TTFT). Decode tasks are fairly scheduled to maintain stable throughput (TPOT).
  • Pipeline Parallelism: Prefill and decode tasks are executed in parallel to ensure decoding is not idle and prefill resources are not blocked.

Complex Implementation

The framework and task scheduler need to support operations such as pipelining, KV Cache output, and asynchronous execution, making it more difficult to maintain than single-machine, single-GPU LLM inference.

Solution:

  • Support for pipelining and asynchronous execution: The task scheduler can manage the overlapping execution of prefill and decode, avoiding idle waiting and blocking.
  • KV Cache output management: This ensures the correct transmission and use of cached data, improving the stability of LLM inference optimization across GPUs and nodes.

High Memory Usage

The prefill phase may process multiple requests simultaneously, and the decode phase requires retaining the entire KV cache, which can put significant memory pressure.

Solution:

  • Tiered storage: Keeps hot data in GPU memory and warm/cold data in host memory or NVMe, reducing memory usage.
  • Memory pool management: Reuses memory to reduce dynamic allocation overhead.
  • RDMA acceleration: Fast KV cache transfers in distributed scenarios, alleviating memory pressure during LLM prefill and LLM decode

Conclusion

Prefill decode separation helps make LLM inference faster and more efficient. By running Prefill and Decode on separate resources, it reduces delays, improves throughput, and optimizes memory use. As models grow, this approach becomes key to keeping AI systems responsive and scalable, paving the way for more reliable large-scale LLM inference optimization.

item.title: KV Cache Offload Accelerates LLM Inference
item.description:
During inference on large language models (LLMs), KV Cache is a key mechanism for improving efficiency. In the Transformer architecture , it stores historical information about attention calculations, thus avoiding repeated computations. However, as context length increases, the amount of video memory occupied by KV Cache rapidly expands, creating a resource bottleneck. To address this, the industry has developed KV Cache Offload technology, which transfers part of the cache to external storage such as CPU memory, achieving memory optimization. This reduces video memory pressure while ensuring inference performance.

1. What is KV Cache?

KV Cache is an acceleration technology. It significantly improves the speed and efficiency of text generation by preserving important information from previous steps and reusing previously calculated results, avoiding recalculation each time. If the Large Language Model (LLM) is like a novelist, then the Transformer is his or her “thinking method.” When a writer writes a story, he or she considers the previous content and decides what to write next. Similarly, when generating text, the Transformer references previously generated tokens and predicts the next token.

Let’s start with the Transformer. In the Transformer’s self-attention mechanism, each token (which can be thought of as the encoding of a word or character) generates three vectors:

  • Q (Query) : What information am I looking for?
  • K (Key) : Who am I? (Feature coordinates)
  • V (Value) : What content do I bring? (Feature content)

When a Transformer generates text, it generates only one new token at a time. The model then compares the Q of this token with the K of all previous tokens to find correlations, and then uses these correlations, V, to calculate the output. However, during inference, a standard Transformer generates data token by token. For example:

  • Input: “I want to read a”
  • Generates: “I want to read a book”
  • Continues to generate: “I want to read a book”

But when generating the third token, it recalculates K and V for all previous tokens, even if these data have already been calculated. This repeated calculation causes the computational complexity of inference to grow quadratically with the number of tokens, making it very slow.

The core principle of KV Cache is to avoid duplication of effort. The core idea of KV Cache is to store historical K and V calculations directly, eliminating the need to recalculate them each time.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

In this way, each time a new token is generated, only the new token’s K and V need to be calculated, added to the previously cached results, and the new token can be generated again using the cached value. This reduces the computational complexity of inference from quadratic to linear, significantly accelerating the process.

2. Why is KV Cache important for Transformer Architecture?

In LLM, KV Cache has become a key mechanism for the efficient operation of the Transformer architecture. It not only solves the computational redundancy problem caused by long contexts but also directly contributes to latency control and throughput improvement. Its importance is primarily reflected in the following aspects:

  • Reduced computational overhead: Avoiding repeated attention calculations on historical sequences reduces inference complexity from quadratic to approximately linear.
  • Improved inference speed: Significantly reduces latency when generating long texts, improving the user experience.
  • Enhanced resource utilization: Reduces unnecessary GPU memory and computing power consumption, providing space for memory optimization.
  • Supports scalability and optimization: Lays the foundation for solutions such as KV Cache Offload, which can move part of the cache to CPU memory, alleviating GPU memory pressure while maintaining performance.

3. What is KV Cache Offload and what does it do?

While KV Cache accelerates Transformer inference, it also has a side effect: it consumes significant graphics memory. This problem becomes increasingly prominent as the context length of large language models continues to grow. For example, when the context length reaches 80,000 tokens, each token must store its corresponding key and value. The KV Cache alone can consume tens of GB of graphics memory. Importantly, GPU graphics memory must accommodate not only the KV Cache but also the model weights and intermediate calculation results. Once this resource is full, inference will crash or even fail.

To address this conflict, the industry has proposed KV Cache Offload. Its core idea is to migrate inactive or temporarily unused KV data from GPU memory to other storage media, such as:

  • CPU memory (RAM): Large capacity, suitable for storing large caches, but with lower bandwidth and latency than GPUs.
  • SSD: Provides massive storage capacity, but is slower and is typically used as a supplementary solution in extreme scenarios.
  • High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM): Its speed approaches that of GPU memory, but it’s expensive and has limited capacity.

Offloading frees up GPU memory to prioritize the smooth operation of ongoing inference. When needed, the system then moves relevant key-value data from external storage back to the GPU. While this incurs some data transfer overhead, offloading is crucial in large-scale inference deployments compared to inference interruptions caused by memory exhaustion. It’s essentially a memory optimization strategy that strikes the optimal balance between performance and resource utilization.

Conclusion

KV Cache Offload technologies have become key to optimizing Transformer-based model inference. By caching historical attention states, KV Cache significantly reduces redundant computation and accelerates response times. Offload strategies, on the other hand, intelligently migrate data between GPUs, CPUs, and even storage tiers to meet the growing memory requirements of large models. These combined technologies not only improve efficiency and scalability, but also pave the way for deploying larger-scale language models with longer context windows. As research and engineering practices continue to advance, the combination of KV Cache Offload will remain a cornerstone of high-performance LLM services.

item.title: Understanding the Prefill-decode Disaggregation in LLM Inference Optimization
item.description:
Introduction

Large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly complex, demanding more efficient inference strategies. One important ai optimization is the prefill decode disaggregation, which decouples the two key stages of inference. Prefill handles the bulk processing of input context, while LLM decode focuses on generating tokens one by one. By separating these stages, we can better utilize hardware resources, reduce latency, and support longer contexts — making large-scale inference optimization more practical and scalable.

1. What is the Prefill and Decode?

In the reasoning process of the Large Language Model (LLM), there are usually two stages:

Prefill Phase (Input Processing)

During this phase, the model reads and processes the entire input text in parallel, breaking it down into tokens and building a holistic understanding of the context. It also generates a key-value (KV) cache to support subsequent generation.

This process is centered around highly parallelized matrix operations, which are computationally intensive but relatively low in memory usage. It is well-suited for running on high-throughput GPUs or multi-GPU systems, efficiently processing input data.

Decode Phase (Output Generation)

During the LLM decode phase, the model enters an autoregressive generation mode: it predicts and outputs new tokens one by one based on the key-value (KV) cache stored in the LLM prefill phase and the previously generated content. Each new token generated depends on the previous result, making the entire process strictly sequential.

The computational model in this phase is completely different from that of the prefill phase. Compared to the highly parallel large-matrix computations of prefill, decode is more like “word-by-word writing,” generating tokens one by one, making it highly sensitive to latency. As the context expands, the KV cache continues to grow, causing GPU memory usage to rapidly increase.

For this reason, the LLM decode phase is typically much slower than prefill and often underutilizes the GPU. In traditional deployments, the prefill and decode phases often share the same GPUs. However, since the LLM prefill phase prioritizes high throughput computing while the decode phase emphasizes low latency and large GPU memory, mixing them together often limits the performance of both.

2. What is the Prefill–Decode Disaggregation?

As the name suggests, prefill decode disaggregation deploys the two stages of the model in different hardware resource pools to better match their respective computing characteristics.

The prefill stage focuses on high-throughput computing and therefore uses GPUs with high computing power and suitable for large-scale parallel computing. The decode stage, on the other hand, is latency-sensitive and requires larger video memory to store the growing amount of context information. Therefore, GPUs with larger video memory and faster response times are used, thereby improving overall LLM inference optimization efficiency.

3. The advantages of the Prefill-Decode Disaggregation

In traditional architectures, the LLM prefill and LLM decode stages share the same device, making them prone to interference. Prefill requests can occupy decode resources, leading to increased latency and output lag. Furthermore, the two stages are suited to different parallelization strategies: prefill is suited to tensor parallelism to reduce latency, while decode is suited to pipeline parallelism to increase throughput. This mixed operation is inefficient.

Get NADDOD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

This interference significantly increases the proportion of high-latency decode requests and forces increased hardware investment. Prefill decode separation, by physically separating the two stages, enables more efficient resource utilization and smoother responses.

  • Dedicated Resource Allocation: Prefill and decode can be independently scheduled and scaled on different hardware. For workloads with high overlap (such as multi-turn conversations or proxy processes), large portions of the KV cache can be reused, reducing prefill computational requirements and freeing up resources for the decode phase.
  • Parallel Execution: Prefill and decode no longer block each other and can run simultaneously, achieving higher concurrency and overall throughput.
  • Independent Optimization: Different LLM optimization strategies (such as tensor parallelism or pipeline parallelism) can be applied to prefill and decode, respectively, to better meet the goals of reducing time to first token (TTFT) and LLM inference latency (ITL).

4. The Challenges of Prefill-Decode Separation

Additional Communication Overhead

If LLM prefill and LLM decode are performed on different GPUs, the KV cache must be transferred, and network latency and bandwidth will become bottlenecks.

Solution:

  • High-performance network: Use ultra-low latency, high-bandwidth networks to reduce KV Cache transmission latency between prefill and decode nodes.
  • KV Cache partitioning: Store data in token-based shards, maximizing data proximity to decode nodes to reduce cross-node transmission.

Increased Scheduling Complexity

The system must coordinate the allocation of tasks between the two columns in real time. Otherwise, the LLM decode phase might wait in vain for the prefill results, or the prefill resources might be blocked by the decoding phase.

Solution:

  • Priority Scheduling: LLM Prefill tasks are prioritized to ensure low latency to first token (TTFT). Decode tasks are fairly scheduled to maintain stable throughput (TPOT).
  • Pipeline Parallelism: Prefill and decode tasks are executed in parallel to ensure decoding is not idle and prefill resources are not blocked.

Complex Implementation

The framework and task scheduler need to support operations such as pipelining, KV Cache output, and asynchronous execution, making it more difficult to maintain than single-machine, single-GPU LLM inference.

Solution:

  • Support for pipelining and asynchronous execution: The task scheduler can manage the overlapping execution of prefill and decode, avoiding idle waiting and blocking.
  • KV Cache output management: This ensures the correct transmission and use of cached data, improving the stability of LLM inference optimization across GPUs and nodes.

High Memory Usage

The prefill phase may process multiple requests simultaneously, and the decode phase requires retaining the entire KV cache, which can put significant memory pressure.

Solution:

  • Tiered storage: Keeps hot data in GPU memory and warm/cold data in host memory or NVMe, reducing memory usage.
  • Memory pool management: Reuses memory to reduce dynamic allocation overhead.
  • RDMA acceleration: Fast KV cache transfers in distributed scenarios, alleviating memory pressure during LLM prefill and LLM decode

Conclusion

Prefill decode separation helps make LLM inference faster and more efficient. By running Prefill and Decode on separate resources, it reduces delays, improves throughput, and optimizes memory use. As models grow, this approach becomes key to keeping AI systems responsive and scalable, paving the way for more reliable large-scale LLM inference optimization.

item.title: Differences between InfiniBand and Ethernet Networks
item.description:
What Is InfiniBand and Ethernet?

InfiniBand and Ethernet, as two important interconnection technologies, have their own characteristics and differences, and can not be generalized to say which is better or worse. They continue to develop and evolve in their different fields of application, and become indispensable in our network world.

InfiniBand is an open standard high-bandwidth, low-latency, and highly reliable network interconnection technology. The technology is defined by IBTA (InfiniBand Trade Alliance) to drive a wide range of applications in supercomputer clusters and, with the rise of artificial intelligence, is also the network interconnection technology of choice for GPU servers. Currently, the latest InfiniBand product is 1.6T XDR , which can provide end-to-end bandwidth of up to 1.6Tbps to the network, bringing an unparalleled network experience for high-performance computing, artificial intelligence and other areas, unleashing the maximum computing potential within the cluster.

Ethernet refers to the baseband LAN specification standard created by Xerox and jointly developed by Xerox, Intel and DEC. The common Ethernet standard was introduced on September 30, 1980, and is the most common communication protocol standard used in today’s existing LANs Currently, the IEEE 802.3 standards organization organized by the IEEE The development of its technical standards, which have been released for 100GE, 200GE, and 400GE Ethernet interfaces, is the highest rate transmission technology available.

What Are the Differences Between InfiniBand and Ethernet?

It goes without saying that Ethernet and IP technology form the cornerstone of the entire interconnection building in the world, and all people and intelligent devices rely on Ethernet to realize the interconnection of everything, which is related to its original design intention of achieving better compatibility and allowing different systems to interconnect better. It has become a de facto standard in the Internet after decades of development.

InfiniBand, on the other hand, was originally developed as a standard to solve the pain point of breaking the data transmission bottleneck in clusters in the high-performance computing scenario, and was positioned for high-end applications from the beginning. InfiniBand is inherently different from Ethernet, mainly in terms of bandwidth, latency, network reliability, and networking methods.

InfiniBand vs Ethernet: Bandwidth

Once born, the development of InfiniBand speed has been faster than that of Ethernet for a long time, mainly because InfiniBand is used for interconnection between servers in high-performance computing, while Ethernet is oriented more to the interconnection of devices at the terminal, and there is no too high demand for bandwidth. Therefore, this leads to the standard organization of Ethernet only to consider how to achieve interoperability when designing the standard. However, Infiniband standard considers not only interoperability but also how to reduce the load on CPU when the network is transmitted at high speed, so that the bandwidth can be used efficiently when communicating at high speed and as few CPU resources as possible are occupied.

For high-speed network traffic of more than 10G, if all messages are processed by the CPU to seal and unseal packets will take up very many resources, which is equivalent to using a large number of resources of expensive CPU resources just to handle the simple task of network transmission, which is a waste from the perspective of resource allocation. Since the rate of the first generation of InfiniBand SDR is 10Gbps, this phenomenon had to be taken into account, and thus, drawing on the idea of DMA technology to bypass the CPU, which not only improves the data transmission bandwidth but also reduces the burden on the CPU. RDMA technology was defined, thus solving the problem and enabling offloading of the CPU in high-speed network transmission while improving the utilization of the network. This allows InfiniBand networks to rapidly iterate from SDR 10Gbps, DDR 20Gbps, QDR 40Gps, FDR 56Gbps, EDR 100Gbps, HDR 200Gbps to today’s NDR 800/400Gbps . Benefiting from RDMA technology, where the CPU does not sacrifice more resources for network processing and slows down the overall HPC due to the significant increase in rate. Faster InfiniBand speeds are in development, like 400Gbps and 800Gbps NDR , and will be launched soon.

InfiniBand vs Ethernet: Latency

We can talk about the latency comparison of InfiniBand and Ethernet in two aspects. One is on the network switch. As a network transport model in the layer 2 technology, Ethernet switches generally use MAC look-up table addressing and store-and-forward (some products borrowed from the InfiniBand Cut-though technology) due to the need to consider such as IP, MPLS, QinQ and other complex services The processing of complex services such as IP, MPLS, QinQ, etc., leads to a long processing process in Ethernet switches, which is usually several us (more than 200ns for those that support cut-through), while InfiniBand switches are very simple to process at layer 2, and only need to look up the forwarding path information based on the 16bit LID, while using Cut-Through.

The second is on network adapter card (NIC). As aforementioned, with RDMA technology, the NIC does not need to go through the CPU to forward messages, which greatly accelerates the delay of message encapsulation and decapsulation processing. Generally, the send/receive latency (write, send) of InfiniBand NICs is 600ns, while the send/receive latency of Ethernet-based TCP UDP applications is around 10us, which is much larder than that of InifniBand.

InfiniBand vs Ethernet: Reliability

In the field of high-performance computing, packet loss and retransmission have a significant impact on the overall performance, so a highly reliable network protocol is needed to ensure the lossless nature of the network from the mechanism level, thus realizing its high reliability characteristics. This not only achieves a truly lossless network, but also makes it possible for the service stream to be transmitted on InfiniBand networks without cache accumulation because there is no congestion on the network, so that delay jitter is controlled to a minimum, thus creating an ideal and pure network.

However, the network constructed by Ethernet does not have a flow control mechanism based on scheduling. As a result, it is impossible to guarantee whether the peer end will be congested when the message is sent out. Therefore, in order to absorb the sudden increase of instantaneous traffic in the network, it is necessary to open a The cache space of dozens of MB is used to temporarily store these messages, and the implementation of the cache occupies a lot of chip area, which makes the chip area of the Ethernet switch with the same specification significantly larger than the InfiniBand chip, which not only costs more but also consumes more power. In addition, due to the lack of an end-to-end flow control mechanism, the network may lose packets due to buffer congestion in slightly extreme cases, causing large fluctuations in data forwarding performance.

InfiniBand vs Ethernet: Networking

The Ethernet networking method requires IP with ARP protocol to automatically generate MAC table entries, which requires each server in the network to send messages at regular intervals to ensure real-time updates of table entries. The network bandwidth is seriously wasted, so it is necessary to introduce VLAN mechanism to divide the virtual network and limit the size of the network, while the inadequacy of the table entry learning mechanism of Ethernet network itself will lead to loop network, and it is necessary to introduce protocols such as STP to ensure that the network forwarding path will not be looped, which increases the complexity of the network configuration. At the same time, with the rise of SDN technology, Ethernet networks are not genetically equipped with SDN traits because they were originally set up with the concept of compatibility, resulting in the need to change the message format (VXLAN) or the forwarding mechanism of the switch (openflow）in such a way that SDN deployments on ethernet networks need to be reworked to meet the requirements of SDN. This is inherent to the SDN concept for InfiniBand, where each InfiniBand layer 2 network has a subnet manager to configure the IDs of the nodes in the network (LocalID), and then the forwarding path information is calculated uniformly through the control plane and sent down to the InfiniBand exchange, so that the formation of an InfiniBand Layer 2 network requires any configuration to complete the network configuration, while no flooding problems, but also eliminates the operation of VLAN and ring breaking, you can easily deploy a large Layer 2 network with a scale of tens of thousands of servers. This is something that Ethernet cannot have.

Although the above perspectives are illustrated by the advantages of InfiniBand, it is undeniable that Ethernet itself has a broad rate support from 10Mbps to 800Gbps and more low-cost equipment support, making it widely used in most scenarios. Since its compatibility extends RDMA technology (RoCE), it makes not only high bandwidth, but also can initially have the qualities of lossless network, therefore, Ethernet network also appears in the field of high-performance computing in recent years, which also shows superb adaptability of Ethernet network from one side.

InfiniBand or RoCE: Which Network Should You Choose?

Choosing the right network for your data center can be a inportant question. For single-machine multi-card setups, communication is usually handled through PCIE or NVLINK within the machine, while multiple-machine multi-card setups require choosing between InfiniBand (IB) or RoCE networks. To support large-scale computing power, super-large-scale clusters require high-speed communication within the machine and low-latency, high-bandwidth communication between machines.

According to user statistics from the Top500 supercomputing centers, IB networks play a significant role in the top 10 and top 100 centers. OpenAI, for example, used an IB network built within Microsoft Azure to train ChatGPT, which has led to a surge in demand for large-scale supercomputing centers.

On the other hand, Microsoft Azure is also a pioneer of the ECQCN algorithm in RoCE and has years of practical engineering experience in building large RoCE networks using Sonic white-box.

Why InfiniBand is the Superior Choice Compared to RoCE

When it comes to choosing a high-speed network for your data center, InfiniBand (IB) and RoCE are two of the most popular options. However, InfiniBand has several advantages that make it the superior choice:

Low Latency

InfiniBand has a dedicated end-to-end network, with each IB switch having a latency of approximately 150–200ns, which is significantly lower than traditional Ethernet switches (500ns or more). This latency advantage becomes even greater in large networks with multiple hops.

Original Lossless Network

Both IB and RoCE are extensions of RDMA applications, but InfiniBand’s point-to-point Credit CC congestion control makes it easy to achieve a lossless network. In contrast, RoCE’s end-to-end flow control and congestion control significantly increase network complexity and maintenance costs.

Hardware Acceleration

IB switches and network cards have unique optimization technologies such as SHARP, Adaptive Routing, and Shield, which give them a significant advantage over Ethernet switches in HPC and AI scenarios, especially for large-scale deployments.

More Flexible Physical Topology

An independent IB subnet can directly support 40,000 nodes without broadcast storms and can support physical topologies such as Fat Tree, DragonFly, DragonFly+, and Torus3D. Even Google’s Aquila network, released in 2022, uses DragonFly’s networking approach.Fat Tree can form a highly efficient and easy-to-expand 2-layer-3-hop or 3-layer-5-hop super topology, with no limit on the scale of network clusters. Therefore, it remains the most widely used topology for both IB and Ethernet networks.

The Pros and Cons of InfiniBand for Your Data Center
